problem_statement.Title: autodoc :inherited-members: option accepts only one class name; does not support multiple classes
problem_statement.Description: The autodoc extension's :inherited-members: option currently accepts either (a) no explicit value / True (treated as a sentinel) or (b) a single class name string. When a class name string is provided, the code uses equality comparison (cls.__name__ == options.inherited_members) to decide whether to treat inherited members from that base class as filtered/ignored. This prevents specifying multiple base classes (e.g., "list,tuple") to ignore their inherited members.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The option parsing function inherited_members_option (sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py) returns: - 'object' when arg is None or True (legacy sentinel), - the raw arg otherwise. It does not parse comma-separated lists into multiple class names, so multi-class input remains a single string (possibly containing commas/whitespace). Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py, function inherited_members_option.

2) The member-filtering logic (inner function is_filtered_inherited_member inside Documenter.filter_members in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py) compares cls.__name__ == self.options.inherited_members. This equality comparison assumes options.inherited_members is a single string class name. It does not handle the case where options.inherited_members is a collection of class names. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py, inner function is_filtered_inherited_member used by Documenter.filter_members.

3) Because inherited_members_option and the equality-based check are coupled, multi-class input like "list,tuple" will not match any single cls.__name__ by equality and therefore the intended classes will not be treated as ignored. The correct relationship is that options.inherited_members should be treated as either: (a) False/Falsey -> no inherited members included; (b) a sentinel (legacy 'object' string) or any truthy value -> include inherited members; and (c) if class-name(s) are supplied, treat that value as either a single class-name string or a collection of class-name strings and perform membership testing (cls.__name__ == option OR cls.__name__ in option).
StepsToReproduce: Create a module with multiple classes that inherit from builtins or from several base classes (for example, classes that inherit from list and tuple).
Use automodule/autoclass with the option :inherited-members: list,tuple (comma-separated list of base class names to ignore).
Build the documentation and observe that members inherited from list and tuple are NOT ignored (unexpected) because the option was treated as a single string.
Alternatively, pass a single class name (e.g., :inherited-members: list) and observe that those members are filtered as expected.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The :inherited-members: option should accept either: (a) no explicit value / True (legacy sentinel) to mean include inherited members, (b) False to mean do not include inherited members, (c) a single class name string to include inherited members but ignore those inherited from that named base class, or (d) a comma-separated list of class names (e.g., "list,tuple") to include inherited members but ignore those inherited from any of the named base classes. When multiple class names are given, members originating from any of those base classes should be filtered/ignored.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The option parser currently does not parse comma-separated lists; it returns the raw string or the legacy sentinel. The filtering code compares base-class names by equality against that single value (cls.__name__ == options.inherited_members). As a result, providing multiple class names (comma-separated) does not cause members from those classes to be ignored; only single-name cases work as intended.

For example, there is a class that inherits multiple base classes:
```
class MyClass(Parent1, Parent2, Parent3, ...):
    pass
```
and

```
.. autoclass:: example.MyClass
   :inherited-members: Parent2
```

How should the new `:inherited-members:` work? Do you mean that the member of Parent2 are ignored and the Parent1's and Parent3's are documented? And how about the methods of the super classes of `Parent1`?

Note: The current behavior is ignoring Parent2, Parent3, and the super classes of them (including Parent1's also). In python words, the classes after `Parent2` in MRO list are all ignored.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_typehints duplicates starred varargs/kwargs in parameter/type lists
problem_statement.Description: When autodoc_typehints is set to "description", var-positional (*args) and var-keyword (**kwargs) parameters are rendered twice: once using the starred name (as written in the docstring) and once unstarred (the annotation key). The duplication appears in the generated Parameters/type lists and in the documented signature block.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The parsing logic in sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py records docstring field names exactly as written (including leading '*' or '**') but the stored annotations use signature parameter names without stars (e.g. 'args' / 'kwargs'). Code locations: modify_field_list (sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py) and augment_descriptions_with_types (sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py). This mismatch ("*args" != "args") causes both starred and unstarred entries to be present.

2) modify_field_list: when adding inferred 'type' and 'param' fields it uses the annotation key (unstarred) as the display name, not the original starred docstring name. Location: modify_field_list in sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py. Current behavior: new fields are created as 'type args' or 'param args' while the docstring contains 'param *args', producing duplicated entries. Correct relationship: when a docstring supplied the starred form, added fields should use that original starred form; the annotation key should be used as the normalized lookup key only (i.e., display_name = raw_name if present else annotation_name).

3) augment_descriptions_with_types: it detects whether parameters already have a 'type' or 'param' by comparing raw parsed names, again without normalizing leading '*' characters. Location: augment_descriptions_with_types in sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py. Current assumption: raw field text equals annotation keys. Correct relationship: normalized_name = raw_name.lstrip('*') should be used for comparisons against annotation keys; preserve raw_name (starred) for display when adding missing 'type' fields.
StepsToReproduce: Create a class with an __init__ signature that includes *args and **kwargs and docstring params that use starred names, for example:
class _ClassWithDocumentedInitAndStarArgs:
def __init__(self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) -> None:
"""Init docstring.
:param x: Some integer
:param *args: Some integer
:param **kwargs: Some integer
Enable the Sphinx autodoc extension and set autodoc_typehints = "description".
Build the documentation (e.g., sphinx-build).
Observe the generated parameter/type lists for duplication of *args/**kwargs.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated documentation shows only the starred parameter names (as written in the docstring/signature) and their corresponding type entries once. For the example, the Parameters/type lists should show '*args' and '**kwargs' (starred forms) with their types/descriptions only once each.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated documentation contains duplicate entries for var-positional and var-keyword parameters: the starred docstring entries (e.g. '*args', '**kwargs') and additional unstarred entries (e.g. 'args', 'kwargs') inserted by typehints. The type/description items for the starred entries are incomplete or missing while the unstarred items may be present, causing confusing duplicates.

I noticed this docstring causes warnings because `*` and `**` are considered as mark-up symbols:

```
    def __init__(self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) -> None:
        """Init docstring.

        :param x: Some integer
        :param *args: Some integer
        :param **kwargs: Some integer
        """
```

Here are warnings:
```
/Users/tkomiya/work/tmp/doc/example.py:docstring of example.ClassWithDocumentedInitAndStarArgs:6: WARNING: Inline emphasis start-string without end-string.
/Users/tkomiya/work/tmp/doc/example.py:docstring of example.ClassWithDocumentedInitAndStarArgs:7: WARNING: Inline strong start-string without end-string.
```

It will work fine if we escape `*` character like the following. But it's not officially recommended way, I believe.

```
    def __init__(self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) -> None:
        """Init docstring.

        :param x: Some integer
        :param \*args: Some integer
        :param \*\*kwargs: Some integer
        """
```

I'm not sure this feature is really needed?

problem_statement.Title: rst_prolog causes top-level headings that start with domain-role expressions (e.g. :mod:`...`) to be misparsed and omitted from toctree
problem_statement.Description: When rst_prolog is set, source files whose first heading line is a domain-role expression (for example ':mod:`mypackage`') are treated incorrectly: the heading is not rendered as a top-level title and is not included in the generated toctree/index.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/util/rst.py — incorrect docinfo detection regex: the module defines
   docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?')
   This pattern matches any line beginning with ':' + word + ':' followed by anything, so it matches domain-role/inline-role expressions like ':mod:`name`'. It therefore misclassifies those lines as docinfo (field list) entries even when they are actually inline role content used as a title. The correct relationship for a docinfo/field marker is that the second colon is followed by whitespace or end-of-line, not an immediate punctuation character. The intended/diagnostic value is:
   docinfo_re = re.compile(r'^:\\w+:(?:\\s|$)')

2) sphinx/util/rst.py — logic in prepend_prolog depends on docinfo_re to find leading docinfo lines. Because the regex misclassifies domain-role-like title lines as docinfo, prepend_prolog advances the insertion position past that line, inserts the rst_prolog after it and also inserts generated blank lines. Those inserted blank lines and the shifted insertion point alter Docutils' section-title detection, so a title whose text is a domain-role expression is no longer recognized as the document's top-level heading and is excluded from the toctree/index. (prepend_prolog is the function that performs the insertion and is the code location that encodes this behavior.)
StepsToReproduce: sphinx-quickstart --no-sep --project mypackage --author me -v 0.1.0 --release 0.1.0 --language en docs

echo -e 'Welcome
=======

.. toctree::

   mypackage
' > docs/index.rst

echo -e ':mod:`mypackage2`
=================

Content

Subheading
----------
' > docs/mypackage.rst

printf "rst_prolog = """
.. |psf| replace:: Python Software Foundation
"""
" >> docs/conf.py
sphinx-build -b html docs docs/_build
Inspect docs/_build/index.html (or search) to see that 'mypackage2' is not rendered as the top-level heading and is not included in the toctree
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: A document whose top-level title text is a domain-role expression (for example ':mod:`mypackage2`') renders that line as the page's top-level heading and that page appears in the project's toctree/index when rst_prolog is set.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When rst_prolog is set, a top-of-file title line that is a domain-role expression is treated as a docinfo/field, the rst_prolog is inserted after it (with additional generated blank lines), and the title is not recognized or rendered as the document's top-level heading and is omitted from the toctree/index.

problem_statement.Title: autosummary: template variable 'members' includes imported members despite autosummary_imported_members = False
problem_statement.Description: When autosummary generates module template contexts, the template variable members contains names of imported attributes (e.g. 'os') even when the configuration autosummary_imported_members is set to False. This manifests in generated .rst files produced by autosummary module templates.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) In sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py, function generate_autosummary_content: for doc.objtype == 'module' the code unconditionally sets ns['members'] = dir(obj). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the raw dir(obj) is an appropriate 'members' list regardless of the autosummary_imported_members config. Current (wrong) assignment: ns['members'] = dir(obj).

2) The correct relationship for 'members' (as indicated by how per-kind lists are computed) depends on the imported_members flag. The intended semantics are: if imported_members is True then members should be dir(obj); otherwise members should be filtered to include only names whose attribute values belong to the module being documented. Expressed as a formula: 
if imported_members:
    ns['members'] = dir(obj)
else:
    ns['members'] = [name for name in dir(obj) if getattr(value_of(obj, name), '__module__', None) == obj.__name__]
The implementation must mirror get_members' rule (use safe_getattr and skip attributes that raise AttributeError) so attributes are accessed safely.

3) The mismatch above is encoded in generate_autosummary_content while get_members(obj, ..., imported=imported_members) already applies the imported_members filter for the per-kind lists (ns['functions'], ns['classes'], ns['exceptions']). This inconsistency (members unfiltered vs. per-kind lists filtered) creates templates-visible divergence: custom templates iterating over members see imported items even when other lists do not.

4) In sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py (setup), the config value is registered with app.add_config_value('autosummary_imported_members', [], False, [bool]) using an empty list [] as the default value. That encodes an incorrect/default typing assumption; the documented/default semantics expect a boolean False. (This is a separate encoding issue for the config default.)
StepsToReproduce: Create _templates/autosummary/module.rst that iterates over the template variable 'members' and renders each item.
Create example.py containing 'import os'.
Create index.rst with an autosummary entry for 'example' and conf.py with autosummary_generate = True and autosummary_imported_members = False.
Build the docs (e.g. sphinx-build ...) and inspect generated/example.rst; observe that the 'members' list contains imported names such as 'os' and other attributes.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When autosummary_imported_members is False, the template variable 'members' supplied to module templates should exclude members that are imported from other modules; it should contain only the module's own members (i.e. names whose attribute value has __module__ equal to the module's __name__). Per-kind lists (functions, classes, exceptions, attributes) should remain consistent with this setting.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated module template context sets members to dir(module) unconditionally, so custom module templates that iterate over members list imported items (e.g. 'os') and various dunder attributes even though autosummary_imported_members is False. Example generated output contained entries like '__builtins__', '__file__', '__name__', and 'os'.

problem_statement.Title: napoleon: trailing underscore in attribute name is over-escaped (visible backslash in HTML)
problem_statement.Description: When napoleon renders an Attributes section for a name that ends with an underscore (e.g. "hello_"), the generated HTML shows the visible backslash ("hello\_") because napoleon inserts a backslash escape into the emitted reST for the attribute directive.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Global parse-time escaping of trailing underscores: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py contains logic that escapes trailing underscores (implements name.endswith('_') -> name = name[:-1] + r'\_') in the name-normalization/consumption phase. This occurs in GoogleDocstring._consume_field and NumpyDocstring._consume_field. That transformation stores an escaped name into the parsed fields and is reused in all later emissions.  
2) Wrong application scope for the escape: the escaping is applied unconditionally at parse time so the escaped form is used both for docutils field labels (where a backslash is required) and for directive signatures like ".. attribute:: NAME" (where the directive should receive the raw name). Location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py — parsing functions GoogleDocstring._consume_field and NumpyDocstring._consume_field encode the incorrect assumption that parsed names should be escaped immediately.  
3) Emission-time requirement differs by context: docutils-field contexts (the left-hand side of :param:, :type:, :ivar:, :vartype:, etc.) require escaping of a trailing underscore so docutils parses the field name correctly; directive signature contexts (e.g. ".. attribute:: NAME", ".. method:: NAME") must receive the raw Python identifier and must not contain the backslash. The correct escaping relationship is: when emitting docutils-style field labels, use escaped_name = name[:-1] + r'\_' if name.endswith('_') (and also escape leading '*' / '**' as r'\*' or r'\*\*' when appropriate). When emitting directive signatures, use the original raw name (no backslash inserted).  
4) Locations that must change/are currently encoding the incorrect assumption:  
   - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py: GoogleDocstring._consume_field (parsing stage) — currently calls the escape helper and stores escaped name.  
   - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py: NumpyDocstring._consume_field (parsing stage) — same issue.  
   - sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py: emission sites that must apply escaping only when needed are _format_docutils_params (for :param/:type/:keyword/:kwtype) and _parse_attributes_section when producing :ivar/:vartype; attribute directive emission (".. attribute:: " + name) must use the raw name.  
5) Preservation of star-escaping: the existing code also handles escaping '*' and '**' by prefixing with backslashes. That behavior should be retained, but applied only in the contexts that require docutils field-label escaping (not globally at parse time).
StepsToReproduce: Create an empty __init__.py in the working directory.
Create a.py with the following contents:
class A:
Attributes
hello_: int
hi
pass
Run sphinx-quickstart to create a docs tree (or use an existing conf.py).
Add 'sphinx.ext.autodoc' and 'sphinx.ext.napoleon' to extensions in conf.py.
Add '.. autoclass:: a.A' to index.rst (or another rst file used to build html).
From the project root (PYTHONPATH=.): make clean html
Open _build/html/index.html and observe the attribute name rendered as 'hello\_' (visible backslash).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The attribute name appears in the generated HTML exactly as the Python name (e.g. "hello_") with no visible backslash. Docutils field labels (like :param:, :ivar:, :type:) should continue to be emitted in a form that docutils parses correctly.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The attribute name is rendered with a visible backslash ("hello\_") in the HTML output produced by napoleon (the backslash appears in the directive output created for the Attributes section).

problem_statement.Title: Two 'Symbols' sections in HTML index with duplicate anchor when index entries use symbol-leading characters
problem_statement.Description: When building the general index (genindex) HTML, index entries that use symbol-leading characters produce two separate sections both titled 'Symbols'. One 'Symbols' section appears before the letter groups and the other after the letter groups. Both sections use the same HTML anchor id so the top-of-page index contains two 'Symbols' links that both jump to the same location.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Inconsistent classification used when sorting index groups (sphinx/environment/adapters/indexentries.py, IndexEntries.create_index -> keyfunc): the sorting key computation normalizes the group label with unicodedata.normalize('NFD') and lowercasing, strips a leading RIGHT-TO-LEFT MARK, then treats labels whose first character is alphabetic or '_' by prefixing chr(127) to push them after symbol groups. This logic treats an explicit category_key that equals the localized 'Symbols' label as a "letter" group because the label starts with an alphabetic character, causing it to be sorted after letters. Exact behavior: lckey = normalize('NFD', key.lower()); if lckey[0:1].isalpha() or lckey.startswith('_'): lckey = chr(127) + lckey.

2) The create_index grouping uses the category_key (index entry tuple element 5) when present, else computes implicit _('Symbols') for entries whose first character is not a letter/underscore (sphinx/environment/adapters/indexentries.py keyfunc3). Both implicit symbol groups and explicit category_key == _('Symbols') produce the same display title _('Symbols'), so they end up as two separate groups with identical titles.

3) Index entry shape and origin of category_key (sphinx/util/nodes.py: process_index_entry; sphinx/domains/std.py: glossary handling): index entries are tuples (type, value, tid, main, index_key). The fifth element (index_key) is used as the category_key by create_index; some directives (e.g. glossary/classifier) can set index_key explicitly to the localized 'Symbols' label.

4) Anchor generation in the genindex HTML rendering/template (genindex.html / genindex-single.html templates and HTML builder use of group titles) uses the group display title to derive the anchor id. Because both the implicit symbol group and the explicit category_key group have the same display title _('Symbols'), the template produces identical anchor ids for the two separate groups, so both top-of-page links labeled 'Symbols' point to the same anchor.

5) Incorrect assumption encoded: treating the literal category_key string as a normal sorting key (i.e., classifying a group as a "letter" group solely because its display text starts with an alphabetic character) is invalid for the special localized 'Symbols' label. The correct relationship (diagnostic): when category_key equals the localized _('Symbols') label (after Unicode normalization and lowercasing), it should be treated as a symbol group for sorting (i.e., it must not receive the chr(127) prefix that places letter groups after symbol groups).
StepsToReproduce: Create documentation with index entries containing leading symbol characters, for example entries starting with '@', '£', and '←' (e.g., using .. index:: @foo, .. index:: £bar, .. index:: ←baz).
Include some normal word index entries (letter-leading) so letter groups appear between symbol entries in the sorted index.
Build the HTML documentation (Sphinx HTML builder) to generate the general index (genindex).
Open the generated index HTML page and inspect the index headings and the top-of-page index links.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: There is a single 'Symbols' section in the generated HTML general index that contains all entries whose leading character is a non-letter (e.g. '@', '£', '←'). The top-of-page index contains a single 'Symbols' link that jumps to that single 'Symbols' section.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Two separate sections titled 'Symbols' appear in the generated HTML index: one before the letter groups (containing some symbol entries) and one after the letter groups (containing other symbol entries). The top-of-page index shows two 'Symbols' links; both links resolve to the same HTML anchor (both jump to the first 'Symbols' section).

problem_statement.Title: Man builder writes man pages into a flat directory instead of section subdirectories (man1, man3, ...)
problem_statement.Description: The Sphinx man page builder emits all generated man pages directly into the builder output directory (e.g. <build-dir>/man/<name>.<section>), producing a flat layout. Unix man(1) expects man pages organized in section subdirectories (e.g. man/man1/, man/man3/), so the generated layout is not compatible with MANPATH lookups.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) Incorrect filesystem layout assumption in sphinx/builders/manpage.py::ManualPageBuilder.write: the code computes targetname = '%s.%s' % (name, section) and writes to destination_path = os.path.join(self.outdir, targetname). This encodes the assumption that placing all man pages directly under self.outdir is acceptable for MANPATH. The correct relationship is that files must be written under a per-section directory: destination_path = os.path.join(self.outdir, 'man' + str(section), targetname) and that the directory os.path.join(self.outdir, 'man' + str(section)) must be created (e.g. via ensuredir) before writing. 2) Tests assume the flat layout: tests/test_build_manpage.py and tests/test_smartquotes.py assert files exist at app.outdir / '<name>.<section>' (e.g. app.outdir / 'python.1'). Those expectations encode the same incorrect layout; the correct expectation is app.outdir / 'man' + <section> / '<name>.<section>' (e.g. app.outdir / 'man1' / 'python.1'). 3) sphinx/writers/manpage.py is not responsible for layout: it correctly embeds the manual section into the file content but does not control filesystem placement. The defect is limited to where ManualPageBuilder chooses the output path.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Sphinx project configured to build manual pages (use the man builder).
Set up man_pages in conf.py (e.g. ('index', 'python', 'Python', ['Author'], 1)).
Run the man builder, for example: sphinx-build -b man <sourcedir> <builddir>/man
List the contents of the build directory (ls <builddir>/man). Observe files like 'python.1' directly under <builddir>/man rather than under a section subdirectory (e.g. <builddir>/man/man1/python.1).
Try to use MANPATH pointing to <builddir>/man; man will not find the pages because it searches in man1/, man3/, etc.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Generated man pages are organized into section directories under the builder output directory. For example, when building man pages for section 1, files should be placed as <build-dir>/man/man1/<name>.1 (and similarly man3/, man5/, etc.), so the build tree can be used directly with MANPATH.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: All generated man pages are written directly into the single output directory (e.g. <build-dir>/man/<name>.<section>) with no per-section subdirectories. This flat layout is incompatible with the man program's MANPATH search, so man cannot locate the generated pages.

hints_text:
I think that users should copy the generated man file to the appropriate directory. The build directory is not an appropriate directory to manage man pages. So no section directory is needed, AFAIK. I don't know why do you want to set `MANPATH` to the output directory. To check the output, you can give the path to the man file for man command like `man _build/man/sphinx-build.1`. Please let me know your purpose in detail.
From a [separate github thread](https://github.com/flux-framework/flux-core/pull/3033#issuecomment-662515605) that describes the specific use case in some more detail:
> When run in a builddir, `src/cmd/flux` sets `MANPATH` such that `man flux` will display the current builddir version of `flux.1`. This is done so that documentation matches the version of Flux being run.

Essentially, we are trying to make running in-tree look as similar to running an installed version as possible.

---

> I think that users should copy the generated man file to the appropriate directory.

On `make install`, we do have the automake setup to copy the manpages to `$prefix/man/man1`, `$prefix/man/man3`, etc.  This did require some extra work though, since each source file and its destination has to be explicitly enumerated in the automake file.  If the man pages were built into their respective sections, a recursive copy would work too.  Not a huge deal, but just another factor I wanted to bring up.
Understandable. +1 to change the structure of output directory. As commented, it causes a breaking change for users. So I propose you to add a configuration `man_make_section_directory = (True | False)` for migration. During 3.x, it defaults to False, and it will default to True on 4.0 release. What do you think?

>I’m happy to take a crack at implementing a fix, though this change in behavior may break some people who expect everything to appear in a man/ directory.

It would be very nice if you send us a PR :-)

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_typehints ignored for overloaded callables (overloads always show annotations)
problem_statement.Description: When documenting functions or methods that use typing.overload, the autodoc_typehints Sphinx option is not respected for the overload signatures: parameter (and sometimes return) annotations are always rendered in the signature output for overloads regardless of the autodoc_typehints setting.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Overloaded-signature code paths do not apply the autodoc_typehints->show_annotation relationship. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py in FunctionDocumenter.format_signature. Incorrect assumption: when formatting overload signatures the code assumes stringify_signature will be called with the same show_annotation option as non-overloaded signatures. Reality/evidence: analyzer.overloads entries are iterated and stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) is called with kwargs empty (no show_annotation), so annotations are always shown.

2) Same omission for methods. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py in MethodDocumenter.format_signature. Incorrect assumption: overloaded methods will inherit the show_annotation setting used for non-overloaded methods. Reality/evidence: overload signatures are created and stringify_signature is invoked without setting show_annotation; therefore parameter annotations appear even when autodoc_typehints is 'none' or 'description'.

3) Same omission for class-level constructor/method overloads. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py in ClassDocumenter.format_signature (get_overloaded_signatures path). Incorrect assumption: overloaded constructor/method signatures will honor autodoc_typehints. Reality/evidence: overloads are evaluated and stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) is called without show_annotation; parameter annotations are rendered despite autodoc_typehints. (Note: ClassDocumenter already sets return_annotation=Parameter.empty in that path, but parameter annotations remain because show_annotation is not set.)

4) General relationship that is not enforced: when env.config.autodoc_typehints is 'none' or 'description', stringify_signature should be called with show_annotation=False. Evidence: elsewhere (non-overload code paths such as format_args) the code uses kwargs.setdefault('show_annotation', False) when autodoc_typehints is 'none' or 'description'; the overloaded branches do not perform that step, leading to inconsistent behavior.
StepsToReproduce: In conf.py set autodoc_typehints = 'none'.

Create an index.rst that uses autodoc to include the module, e.g.:
.. automodule:: example
   :members:
   :undoc-members:
Build the documentation with Sphinx and observe the rendered signatures for the overloaded callable(s).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The autodoc_typehints setting is applied to all documented callables including overloaded callables: with autodoc_typehints='none' parameter (and return) annotations are not shown in the signature; with 'description' annotations are moved to the description/type fields; with 'signature' annotations are shown in the signature.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: For overloaded functions and methods, annotations in the overload signatures are always shown in the rendered documentation regardless of the autodoc_typehints setting (e.g. autodoc_typehints='none' does not suppress annotations for overloads).

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_type_aliases ignored for module-level variables and class attributes
problem_statement.Description: When documenting module-level variables and class attributes, the autodoc_type_aliases config mapping is not applied; types annotated via forward references (or string annotations) are not resolved using the configured aliases.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py — DataDocumenter.add_directive_header: the code calls typing.get_type_hints(self.parent) without supplying the configured autodoc_type_aliases mapping. This encodes the incorrect assumption that get_type_hints(self.parent) alone will resolve forward/string annotations that require alias mappings. The correct relationship (used elsewhere in the codebase) is to pass the alias mapping as the localns third argument: get_type_hints(self.parent, None, self.config.autodoc_type_aliases).

2) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py — AttributeDocumenter.add_directive_header: same as (1) for class attributes — it calls typing.get_type_hints(self.parent) without the autodoc_type_aliases mapping. This prevents alias-based resolution for class attribute annotations. The correct relationship is get_type_hints(self.parent, None, self.config.autodoc_type_aliases).

3) Fallback behavior: when get_type_hints fails (NameError), the code falls back to using raw __annotations__ or analyzer.annotations (string form). Those textual fallbacks are not evaluated with autodoc_type_aliases, so without successful get_type_hints evaluation the alias mapping will not be applied.
StepsToReproduce:
Create example.py with the following content:

from __future__ import annotations

#: blah blah blah
var: String


class MyString:
    "mystring"

    #: blah blah blah
    var: String
Create index.rst containing:

.. automodule:: example
   :members:
   :undoc-members:
Create conf.py containing:

autodoc_type_aliases = {
    'String': 'example.MyString'
}
Run Sphinx to build the documentation (e.g. sphinx-build -b html . _build) and inspect the documented types for example.var and example.MyString.var.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The documented type for example.var and example.MyString.var should reflect the autodoc_type_aliases mapping (i.e., the annotation 'String' should be resolved/represented according to the mapping, e.g. example.MyString).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: autodoc_type_aliases is not applied to module-level variables and class attributes; their documented types remain the original annotation name (e.g. 'String') or an unresolved forward-reference string instead of the mapped type.

problem_statement.Title: Spurious "no number is assigned for table" warnings for :numref: to uncaptioned tables (Sphinx 3.3)
problem_statement.Description: After upgrading to Sphinx 3.3, builds (singlehtml and latex reported) started emitting warnings: "WARNING: no number is assigned for table: <labelid>" when resolving :numref: for table labels. The warnings are produced during numref resolution inside the standard domain.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) StandardDomain._resolve_numref_xref (sphinx/domains/std.py) determines the figure type (figtype) from the target node's class (tables map to figtype 'table') without checking whether the node actually has a caption/title. 2) The figure-number assignment step (TocTreeCollector.assign_figure_numbers in sphinx/environment/collectors/toctree.py) intentionally skips std-domain nodes that do not have a caption/title (it checks domain.name == 'std' and not domain.get_numfig_title(node) -> continue), so uncaptioned std tables are not present in env.toc_fignumbers. 3) Because the resolver attempts to fetch a fignumber for a table that was never assigned a number, get_fignumber ends up raising ValueError and the resolver logs: "no number is assigned for table: <labelid>". The core incorrect assumption: treating a node as numerable solely by its node class (figtype) in resolution, whereas numbering assignment applies an additional requirement for std-domain nodes (presence of caption/title). The resolver must consider get_numfig_title(target_node) for std nodes before attempting a fignumber lookup. This mismatch is encoded in: - sphinx/domains/std.py: StandardDomain._resolve_numref_xref (attempts number lookup unconditionally for non-section figtypes) The correct relationship (preserved from the investigation): for std-domain nodes, being of a numerable node class (enumerable_nodes mapping) is necessary but not sufficient for numbering; the node must also have a numfig title/caption as determined by StandardDomain.get_numfig_title(target_node). If get_numfig_title(target_node) is None for a std-domain non-section node (e.g. uncaptioned table), the resolver must not attempt to lookup env.toc_fignumbers[docname][figtype][figure_id] (which will be absent) and must not emit the "no number is assigned for ..." warning.
StepsToReproduce: Create a document with a table that has no caption and a label, e.g.:
.. _tbl-uncaptioned:
.. table::
:name: tbl-uncaptioned
In another document, reference it with :numref:`tbl-uncaptioned`.
Build singlehtml or latex with Sphinx 3.3.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: References using :numref: to an uncaptioned table should not produce a "no number is assigned for table" warning. The reference should be left as the original content (no numeric substitution) or otherwise resolved in a way that does not emit a misleading warning about an assigned number.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Sphinx logs: "WARNING: no number is assigned for table: <labelid>" during numref resolution (StandardDomain._resolve_numref_xref), because the resolver attempted to look up a figure number for a std table that was not given a number (assignment skipped due to missing caption).

hints_text: I digged into this a little bit more and it seems like the `id` of the table isn't properly making it into `env.toc_fignumbers`. If I set `:name: mylabel`, regardless the I see something like this in `env.toc_fignumbers` ``` 'pagename': {'table': {'id3': (1,)}, ``` So it seems like `id3` is being used for the table id instead of `mylabel` @choldgraf I suspect it's related to this: https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/commit/66dda1fc50249e9da62e79380251d8795b8e36df. Oooohhh good find! 👏👏👏 Confirmed that this was the issue - we had been referencing Tables that didn't have a title with `numref`, and this bugfix (I guess it was a bugfix?) caused us to start raising errors. Perhaps this restriction about tables needing a title could be documented more clearly? The `numfig` option has been described as follows. >If true, figures, tables and code-blocks are automatically numbered if they have a caption. https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/configuration.html#confval-numfig It says a table not having a title is not assigned a number. Then `numfig` can't refer it because of no table number. > It says a table not having a title is not assigned a number. Then `numfig` can't refer it because of no table number. This means that a user is not able to add a numbered table with no caption correct? I could understand such restrictions for Jupyter Book but it doesn't make a lot of sense for Sphinx IMO. I think Sphinx should allow users to have enumerable nodes with no caption. What do you think @choldgraf? >This means that a user is not able to add a numbered table with no caption correct? Yes. Since the beginning, numfig feature only supports captioned figures and tables. I don't know how many people want to assign numbers to non-captioned items. But this is the first feature request, AFAIK. I think my take is that I don't think it is super useful to be able to have numbered references for things that don't have titles/captions. However, it also didn't feel like it *shouldn't* be possible, and so I assumed that it was possible (and thus ran into what I thought was a bug). I think it would be more helpful to surface a more informative warning like "You attempted to add a numbered reference to a Table without a title, add a title for this to work." (or, surface this gotcha in the documentation more obviously like with a `warning` or `note` directive?) @tk0miya @choldgraf both make good points for restricting `figure` and `table` directives with no caption. My issue is that this is done at the enumerable node which implies that **all** enumerable nodes with no title/caption are skipped - not just `figure` and `table`. > Since the beginning, numfig feature only supports captioned figures and tables. Just to clarify, `numfig` feature has - prior to v3.3.0 - supported uncaptioned tables but it did not display the caption. The user was able to reference the table using `numref` role (see example below). In the event that the user tried to reference the caption (aka `name` placeholder), Sphinx threw a warning indicating that there was no caption. This solution seemed sensible to me because it allowed other extensions to utilize enumerable nodes regardless of caption/no caption restriction. My main motivation for wanting to revert back or restrict the bugfix to tables and figures is because both the extensions I've worked on depend on the utilization of enumerable nodes regardless of captions/no captions. I think it wouldn't be too difficult to add the information to `env.toc_fignumbers` but I wanted to make a case before I addressed this in [sphinx-proof](https://github.com/executablebooks/sphinx-proof) and [sphinx-exercise](https://github.com/executablebooks/sphinx-exercise). **Example** Sphinx Version - v3.2.1 ````md ```{list-table} :header-rows: 1 :name: table1 * - Training - Validation * - 0 - 5 * - 13720 - 2744 ``` Referencing table using `numref`: {numref}`table1`. ```{list-table} Caption here :header-rows: 1 :name: table2 * - Training - Validation * - 0 - 5 * - 13720 - 2744 ``` Referencing table using `numref`: {numref}`table2`. ```` So it sounds like the `tl;dr` from @najuzilu is that in other extensions, she is *using* the fact that you can reference non-captioned elements with a number, and that Sphinx now removing this ability is breaking those extensions. Is that right? That's correct @choldgraf This is a screenshot of the PDF that is generated from @najuzilu 's example with v3.2.1. As you see, it does not work correctly in LaTeX output. I'd not like to support assigning numbers to no captioned items until fixed this (if somebody needs it).

problem_statement.Title: Sphinx 3.2 rejects option:: signatures with bracketed tokens like "[enable=]PATTERN"
problem_statement.Description: Sphinx 3.2 emits a "Malformed option description" warning for option:: directive signatures that include bracketed tokens (for example "[enable=]PATTERN"), rejecting option syntax that earlier Sphinx versions accepted.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) A single, overly restrictive regular expression (option_desc_re) is used to validate option directive signatures. Location: sphinx/domains/std.py, used by Cmdoption.handle_signature. The current pattern is: ((?:/|--|-|\+)?[^\s=[]+)(=?\s*.*). This pattern's [^\s=[]+ forbids '[' and '=' in the option-name group, so signatures beginning with a '[' (e.g. "[enable=]PATTERN") fail to match and are treated as malformed.

2) The regex was written with the explicit assumption that an unbracketed '=' always separates option name and its args (so "--opt=FILE" must split into optname="--opt" and args="=FILE"). That assumption prevented allowing '=' into the first group in all cases, but it did not account for legitimate bracketed forms where '=' appears inside a bracketed token (e.g. "[enable=]"). The correct relationship is that the option-name should allow bracketed tokens (whose contents may include '=') while still splitting an unbracketed '=' as the arg-separator. The investigation-prescribed replacement pattern is: ((?:/|--|-|\+)?(?:\[[^\]]+\]|[^\s=])+)(=?\s*.*).

3) The validation logic is localized to sphinx/domains/std.py (option_desc_re and Cmdoption.handle_signature). No other modules were found to implement the same check, so this single regex is the root encoding of the restrictive assumption.
StepsToReproduce:
Create an RST file containing the following lines:

.. program:: qemu

.. option:: [enable=]PATTERN

   Immediately enable events matching *PATTERN*
Run sphinx-build (Sphinx 3.2) to build the documentation (or run the project's usual doc build that uses the system sphinx-build).
Observe the build failure/warning about a malformed option description.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The option:: directive should accept the signature "[enable=]PATTERN" (and other bracketed option-name forms) without emitting a "Malformed option description" warning; the option should be registered and documented like other valid option signatures.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
Sphinx 3.2 issues the warning (treated as error):

Warning, treated as error:
../../docs/qemu-option-trace.rst.inc:4:Malformed option description '[enable=]PATTERN', should look like "opt", "-opt args", "--opt args", "/opt args" or "+opt args"

hints_text:
I disagree with 

> Sphinx ideally shouldn't change in ways that break the building of documentation that worked in older versions, because this makes it unworkably difficult to have documentation that builds with whatever the Linux distro's sphinx-build is.

The idea that things shouldn't change to avoid breaking is incredibly toxic developer culture. This is what pinned versions are for, additionally, you can have your project specify a minimum and maximum sphinx as a requirement.
I agree that there's some philosophical differences at play here. Our project wants to be able to build on a fairly wide range of supported and shipping distributions (we go for "the versions of major distros still supported by the distro vendor", roughly), and we follow the usual/traditional C project/Linux distro approach of "build with the versions of libraries, dependencies and tools shipped by the build platform" generally. At the moment that means we need our docs to build with Sphinx versions ranging from 1.6 through to 3.2, and the concept of a "pinned version" just doesn't exist in this ecosystem. Being able to build with the distro version of Sphinx is made much more awkward if the documentation markup language is not a well specified and stable target for documentation authors to aim at.

Incidentally, the current documentation of the option:: directive in https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/restructuredtext/domains.html?highlight=option#directive-option says nothing about this requirement for -, --, / or +.

For the moment I've dealt with this by rewriting the fragment of documentation to avoid the option directive. I don't want to get into an argument if the Sphinx project doesn't feel that strong backward-compatibility guarantees are a project goal, so I thought I'd just write up my suggestions/hopes for sphinx-build more generally for you to consider (or reject!) and leave it at that:

* Where directives/markup have a required syntax for their arguments, it would be useful if the documentation clearly and precisely described the syntax. That allows documentation authors to know whether they're using something as intended.
* Where possible, the initial implementation should start with tightly parsing that syntax and diagnosing errors. It's much easier to loosen restrictions or use a previously forbidden syntax for a new purpose if older implementations just rejected it rather than if they accepted it and did something different because they didn't parse it very strictly.
* Where major changes are necessary, a reasonable length period of deprecation and parallel availability of old and new syntax helps to ease transitions.

and on a more general note I would appreciate it if the project considered the needs of external non-Python projects that have adopted Sphinx as a documentation system but which don't necessarily have the same control over tooling versions that Python-ecosystem projects might. (The Linux kernel is another good example here.)

> Where major changes are necessary, a reasonable length period of deprecation and parallel availability of old and new syntax helps to ease transitions.

Major versions are done via semver, where Sphinx 2 is a major breaking change over Sphinx 1, and Sphinx 3 breaks changes over Sphinx 2. What other things could be done? The concept of deprecation isn't as common in Python communities due to the popularity of fixed versions or locking to a major version. IE ``pip install sphinx==3`` which installs the latest major sphinx version of 3.
This change was added at https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/pull/7770. It is not an expected change. It means this is a mere bug.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc treats empty __all__ as absent — modules with __all__ = [] still document members
problem_statement.Description: When a module defines __all__ = [] (explicit empty export list), sphinx.ext.autodoc nevertheless documents the module's members (functions, etc.). The empty __all__ is ignored and autodoc behaves as if __all__ were not defined.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) inspect.getall(obj) returns None when a module has no __all__ attribute and returns the sequence (including an empty list) when __all__ is present. This distinction is relied on by ModuleDocumenter.import_object which sets self.__all__ = inspect.getall(self.object). (Location: sphinx.util.inspect.getall and ModuleDocumenter.import_object in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py.)

2) ModuleDocumenter.get_object_members misinterprets the presence of an explicit empty __all__ because it checks presence by truthiness: it uses "if not self.__all__" to detect "no __all__". An empty list ([]) is falsy in Python, so the code conflates "__all__ absent (None)" and "__all__ present but empty ([])" and falls back to the "no __all__" behavior, causing all members to be included. (Location: ModuleDocumenter.get_object_members in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py; current conditional: "if want_all: if not self.__all__: ... else: ..."; correct relationship: test must distinguish None vs sequence — i.e. treat self.__all__ is None as "no __all__", and any sequence (including []) as an explicit __all__.)

3) ModuleDocumenter.sort_members (bysource ordering branch) also uses a truthiness test to decide whether to apply __all__-based ordering: it uses "if order == 'bysource' and self.__all__:". This treats an explicit empty __all__ as falsy and skips __all__-based ordering when __all__ = [], again conflating absence and presence. (Location: ModuleDocumenter.sort_members in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py; correct relationship: check "self.__all__ is not None" to detect presence of an explicit __all__ sequence, even if empty.)
StepsToReproduce: Create example.py containing: __all__ = [] and three functions foo(), bar(), baz(), each with a docstring.
Create index.rst containing: '.. automodule:: example' and the ':members:' option.
Build the documentation (e.g. sphinx-build -b html . _build).
Observe the generated documentation for the example module.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Because the module explicitly defines __all__ = [], autodoc should document no module members (the empty export list means "export nothing").
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: All functions (foo, bar, baz) are shown in the generated documentation; the explicit empty __all__ is ignored and modules are documented as if __all__ were not defined.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc fails to resolve struct.Struct type annotations (produces 'class reference target not found: Struct')
problem_statement.Description: When a function parameter or return annotation uses struct.Struct, Sphinx autodoc formats the annotation as the bare name 'Struct' (losing the module prefix) which becomes an unresolved cross-reference and triggers the warning "class reference target not found: Struct". Other annotations (e.g. pathlib.Path) are resolved correctly.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect heuristic in sphinx.util.typing.stringify(annotation): it assumes that if annotation.__module__ == 'builtins' and hasattr(annotation, '__qualname__') then the module can be stripped and the bare qualname returned. Location: sphinx/util/typing.py (stringify).  This produces 'Struct' instead of a module-qualified name when that heuristic is wrong.

2) The same incorrect heuristic in sphinx.util.typing.restify(cls): it treats cls.__module__ in ('__builtin__','builtins') as sufficient to omit the module when emitting :class:`...` text. Location: sphinx/util/typing.py (restify).  That yields ':class:`Struct`' as the reST text used for the xref target.

3) A similar assumption in sphinx.ext.inheritance_diagram.class_name: it drops the module for classes whose __module__ is '__builtin__' or 'builtins' without verifying the object is actually the builtin exported from the builtins module. Location: sphinx/ext/inheritance_diagram.py (class_name).

4) Manifestation point: the formatted annotation string (from stringify/restify) is later parsed into a pending_xref whose reftarget is the produced text. Location where the unresolved target warning is generated: sphinx.domains.python's annotation parsing / xref creation code (e.g. codepaths that parse formatted annotation text and create pending_xref). The bare 'Struct' reftarget cannot be resolved, producing the "class reference target not found: Struct" warning.

5) Correct relationship / condition discovered by investigation (used to decide when it is safe to strip the module): do not rely solely on __module__ == 'builtins'. Instead, only omit the module name when the builtins module actually exposes the same object under that name — i.e. getattr(builtins, name, None) is annotation/cls. If that identity test fails, the formatter should not return a bare qualname; it should use a module-qualified name (preferably the defining module as discovered by inspect.getmodule(obj) if available). This exact identity test and the fallback to inspect.getmodule(...) are the relationships discovered and required to avoid producing incorrect bare names.
StepsToReproduce: Create docs/index.rst containing the provided minimal automodule invocation (see report).
Create a module helloworld.py with functions annotated using struct.Struct and pathlib.Path as in the report:
import struct
import pathlib
def consume_struct(_: struct.Struct) -> None: pass
def make_struct() -> struct.Struct: return struct.Struct('HH')
def make_path() -> pathlib.Path: return pathlib.Path()
Run Sphinx to build HTML (example): python3 -m sphinx -b html docs/ doc-out -nvWT
Observe the warning and missing resolved link for Struct (commenting out the struct-annotated functions makes pathlib.Path resolve normally).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: autodoc should render and resolve struct.Struct annotations the same way as other module-qualified types (e.g. show/resolve as 'struct.Struct' or a working cross-reference), and no "class reference target not found: Struct" warning should be emitted.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: autodoc emits a warning "class reference target not found: Struct" and the documentation output contains an unresolved/missing reference for the Struct annotation (while pathlib.Path resolves correctly).

hints_text:
Unfortunately, the `struct.Struct` class does not have the correct module-info. So it is difficult to support.
```
Python 3.8.2 (default, Mar  2 2020, 00:44:41)
[Clang 11.0.0 (clang-1100.0.33.17)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import struct
>>> struct.Struct.__module__
'builtins'
```

Note: In python3.9, it returns the correct module-info. But it answers the internal module name: `_struct`.
```
Python 3.9.1 (default, Dec 18 2020, 00:18:40)
[Clang 11.0.3 (clang-1103.0.32.59)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import struct
>>> struct.Struct.__module__
'_struct'
```

So it would better to use `autodoc_type_aliases` to correct it forcedly.
```
# helloworld.py
from __future__ import annotations  # important!
from struct import Struct

def consume_struct(_: Struct) -> None:
    pass
```
```
# conf.py
autodoc_type_aliases = {
    'Struct': 'struct.Struct',
}
```

Then, it working fine.

problem_statement.Title: napoleon_use_param ignored for "Other parameters" section (GoogleDocstring._parse_other_parameters_section)
problem_statement.Description: The "Other parameters" section is always rendered using the single-block _format_fields style and does not respect the napoleon_use_param configuration, causing inconsistent formatting compared to the "Parameters" section.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) _parse_other_parameters_section ignores the napoleon_use_param configuration and unconditionally calls _format_fields. Location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py, GoogleDocstring._parse_other_parameters_section. 2) NumpyDocstring inherits GoogleDocstring._parse_other_parameters_section, so Numpy-style docstrings are affected as well. Location: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py, NumpyDocstring (no override). 3) The intended relationship (as implemented for Parameters and Receives) is: if self._config.napoleon_use_param is True then fields must be consumed with multiple=True and rendered with _format_docutils_params(fields); otherwise use _format_fields(_('Other Parameters'), fields). This exact conditional and the use of multiple=True when napoleon_use_param is True is missing from _parse_other_parameters_section. 4) _parse_parameters_section and _parse_receives_section already implement the correct conditional behavior and therefore demonstrate the intended pattern to follow. Locations: sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py, GoogleDocstring._parse_parameters_section and GoogleDocstring._parse_receives_section.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL with Sphinx installed (including sphinx.ext.napoleon).
Run the following code:
from sphinx.ext.napoleon import NumpyDocstring
print(str(NumpyDocstring('''
Parameters
x : int
Other parameters
y: float
Observe the rendered output for the "Other parameters" section.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The "Other parameters" section should follow the same formatting rules as the "Parameters" section. When napoleon_use_param is True, each parameter in "Other parameters" should be emitted as separate :param: and :type: entries (allowing comma-separated multiple names). When napoleon_use_param is False, the section should be rendered as a single "Other Parameters" block.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The "Other parameters" section is always rendered as a single block heading (inline bold/type formatting) regardless of the napoleon_use_param setting, resulting in a mismatch with how the "Parameters" section is formatted when napoleon_use_param is True.

problem_statement.Title: viewcode generates module pages for EPUB even when viewcode_enable_epub=False (make html epub)
problem_statement.Description: When running a combined build (e.g. `make html epub`) the viewcode extension creates standalone highlighted module pages for the EPUB build even though conf.py sets viewcode_enable_epub = False. The extra module pages appear in the EPUB output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) collect_pages lacks the same builder-type guard as doctree_read: the function collect_pages in sphinx/ext/viewcode.py is registered on the 'html-collect-pages' event but does not early-return for singlehtml or for epub builders when viewcode_enable_epub is False. This causes module pages to be generated during EPUB builds.

2) doctree_read contains the intended guard but collect_pages does not: doctree_read in sphinx/ext/viewcode.py already returns early for app.builder.name == 'singlehtml' and for app.builder.name.startswith('epub') when not env.config.viewcode_enable_epub. The inconsistent checks encode the incorrect assumption that preventing doctree modifications is sufficient to prevent EPUB module-page generation.

3) EPUB builders trigger html-collect-pages: the EPUB builder inherits/uses the HTML builder page-generation pathway (see sphinx/builders/_epub_base.py and sphinx/builders/html/__init__.py), so collect_pages is invoked for EPUB builds (including when building html and epub in one command). Because collect_pages lacks the guard, it runs and yields pages for EPUB even when viewcode_enable_epub is False.

4) Correct relationship/condition that is missing in collect_pages: collect_pages must skip producing pages when either app.builder.name == 'singlehtml' OR (app.builder.name.startswith('epub') AND not env.config.viewcode_enable_epub). The absence of this condition in collect_pages is the direct cause of the observed behavior.
StepsToReproduce: In conf.py set: viewcode_enable_epub = False
Run a combined build that includes both html and epub, e.g. `make html epub`
Inspect the EPUB build output directory for generated viewcode module pages (e.g. _modules or viewcode-related files)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When viewcode_enable_epub is False, the EPUB build should not include standalone viewcode module pages. Building both HTML and EPUB in one command should produce viewcode pages for HTML (if enabled) but not for EPUB when the config disables it.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Standalone viewcode module pages are generated for the EPUB build even though viewcode_enable_epub is set to False; this occurs when running combined builds such as `make html epub`.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc treats inherited annotation-only superclass attribute as undocumented
problem_statement.Description: When a superclass defines an attribute using PEP-526 annotation syntax and documents it with an inline #: comment, that attribute is not shown under a subclass documented with .. autoclass:: <SubClass> using :members: and :inherited-members:. The attribute appears only if :undoc-members: is given, indicating autodoc treated it as undocumented.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py:get_class_members — ordering bug: annotation-only attributes from getannotations(cls) are added to the members dict before ModuleAnalyzer.attr_docs are applied. The analyzer processing only adds documented attributes when "name not in members", so documented annotation-only attributes that were pre-added end up as ObjectMember entries with object == INSTANCEATTR but without a docstring (ObjectMember.docstring is not set).

2) sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py:get_object_members — the same add-first-then-check-attr_docs pattern exists here as well, making this code path susceptible to the same problem in contexts that use get_object_members.

3) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py:Documenter.filter_members (and related member-filtering logic) determines whether a member is documented by checking getdoc(member) and ObjectMember.docstring. Because the ObjectMember for the annotation-only attribute lacks a docstring (see #1), autodoc treats it as undocumented and skips it unless :undoc-members: is explicitly supplied.

4) Name matching detail: analyzer.attr_docs entries are associated with (qualname, name) pairs and should be compared against members keys using unmangle(cls, name). The observed failure is not a mismatch of unmangling itself but the result of the early insertion of annotation-only placeholders (see #1) which prevents analyzer-provided docstrings from being attached to those placeholders.
StepsToReproduce:
Create example.py with:
class Foo:
    """docstring"""
    attr1: int  #: docstring

class Bar(Foo):
    """docstring"""
    attr2: str  #: docstring

Create index.rst with:
.. autoclass:: example.Bar
   :members:
   :inherited-members:
Build the Sphinx docs (e.g. sphinx-build -b html . _build) and inspect the generated documentation for Bar.
Observe that Foo.attr1 (inherited annotation-only, with inline #: docstring) is not shown under Bar unless :undoc-members: is also added to the autoclass directive.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The inherited attribute defined in the superclass with a PEP-526 annotation and an inline #: docstring (Foo.attr1) should be listed under the subclass (Bar) when using .. autoclass:: Bar with :members: and :inherited-members:, showing its documentation comment.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The inherited annotation-only attribute (Foo.attr1) is omitted from the generated documentation for Bar when using :members: and :inherited-members:. The attribute is shown only if :undoc-members: is added, indicating autodoc considered it undocumented.
